 {"id":"process_triage-68c.1","title":"Add Rust fmt + clippy checks to CI","description":"## Purpose
Add a Rust linting job to `.github/workflows/ci.yml` that enforces baseline code quality for `pt-core`:
- `cargo fmt --check`
- `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`

## Why This Matters
The project’s core safety and numerical correctness live in Rust. Style drift and ignored warnings are a fast path to subtle bugs.

## Scope
- Install a pinned Rust toolchain (stable) in CI.
- Run formatting and clippy against the workspace.
- Ensure feature flags don’t hide warnings:
  - run clippy at least once with the default feature set
  - optionally add a minimal-features run (`--no-default-features`) if the workspace supports it

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CI fails if `cargo fmt --check` fails.
- [ ] CI fails on clippy warnings (warnings treated as errors).
- [ ] Job runs on Linux and macOS.
- [ ] Job uses caching so runtime is reasonable.

## Test Plan
- Open a PR with an intentional fmt/clippy violation and confirm CI blocks it.

 {"id":"process_triage-68c.2","title":"Add cargo test matrix job for pt-core","description":"## Purpose
Add a `cargo test` job to `.github/workflows/ci.yml` that runs `pt-core` unit + integration tests on supported OSes.

## Scope
- Run `cargo test` for the workspace on:
  - ubuntu-latest
  - macos-latest
- Include at least one feature configuration beyond the default (if applicable):
  - e.g., `--features deep,report` (or whichever features exist)
  - ensure we don’t accidentally break optional components

## Guardrails
- Tests must be deterministic and not depend on the real process table by default.
- Tests should rely on fixtures/mocks for `/proc` and system commands.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `cargo test` runs on Linux and macOS for PRs.
- [ ] The job exercises both default features and at least one optional feature set (when available).
- [ ] Failures print useful logs (capture test output; do not swallow).

## Test Plan
- Verify CI runs on a PR and executes all tests.
- Add a deliberately failing test in a branch to confirm the job surfaces logs.

 {"id":"process_triage-6a1","title":"Implement DRO layer for distribution shift","description":"## Overview
Implement **Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO)** as a conservative decision gate under distribution shift / model misspecification.

This is explicitly a **safety layer**: it does not replace the closed-form posterior; it tightens decisions when drift/PPC indicates “the model may be lying.”

## Plan References
- Plan §4.42 (DRO)
- Plan §5.12 (worst-case expected loss)

## Core Idea
Replace nominal expected loss with a **worst-case** expected loss over an ambiguity set around the nominal/baseline distribution.

### Wasserstein DRO (canonical)
```
worst_case_loss = sup_{Q: W(Q,P) ≤ ε}  E_Q[L]
```
Where:
- `P` is the nominal predictive / baseline distribution (from telemetry baselines or posterior predictive)
- `Q` ranges over distributions within a Wasserstein ball of radius `ε`
- `ε` encodes how much shift we want to be robust to

### Dual Form (when available)
For many losses, Wasserstein DRO admits a tractable dual:
```
inf_{λ ≥ 0}  { λ ε + E_P[ sup_q ( L(q) - λ c(q,p) ) ] }
```
Use closed-form dual bounds where possible; otherwise use conservative approximations.

## When DRO Activates
DRO should be applied when any misspecification signal is raised:
- PPC failures (Plan §4.41)
- drift detection triggers (e.g., Wasserstein/Sinkhorn divergence; Plan §4.24)
- robust Bayes “η-tempering” reduced due to mismatch (Plan §4.9 / Safe-Bayes)
- explicit user/policy override: `--conservative`

## How DRO Changes Decisions
- Compute nominal expected loss `E_P[L(a,S)|x]`.
- Compute robust/worst-case expected loss bound for candidate actions.
- If robust bound reverses the decision (e.g., kill becomes worse than keep/pause), **de-escalate**.

Outputs must be visible in:
- plan summary (human)
- evidence ledger / galaxy-brain cards
- agent JSON (machine-readable)

Example agent structure:
```json
{
  "dro": {
    "applied": true,
    "reason": "wasserstein_drift_detected",
    "ambiguity_radius": 0.15,
    "original_action": "kill",
    "robust_action": "review",
    "worst_case_expected_loss": 45.2
  }
}
```

## Ambiguity Radius ε Selection
- Policy-configurable default ε.
- Calibrate ε from historical drift magnitudes (shadow-mode), optionally per host-profile.
- Adapt ε based on confidence/misspecification severity (larger ε → more conservative).

## Deliverables
- Implementation in `decision/robust_loss.rs` (or `inference/dro.rs`, depending on module layout).
- Integration with drift/PPC gates.
- Ledger/telemetry fields for “DRO applied” decisions.
- Unit tests and documentation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Worst-case expected loss gating is implemented and can de-escalate actions conservatively.
- [ ] ε is policy-configurable and logged/telemetried.
- [ ] Trigger conditions (PPC/drift/explicit flag) deterministically enable/disable DRO.
- [ ] Outputs are explainable (reason + bound values surfaced in ledger).

## Test Plan
- Unit: synthetic examples where drift triggers and DRO reverses a kill to a safer action.
- Unit: ε calibration logic and monotonicity (larger ε never decreases worst-case loss).
- Integration: drift detection → DRO gate → plan generation uses robust decision.

-{"id":"process_triage-6b2","title":"Implement pt agent sessions command","description":"## Overview
Implement the `pt agent sessions` command for listing and managing sessions across invocations.

## From Plan Section 3.5.1

### Command Purpose
List, inspect, and manage sessions. Essential for agents tracking multiple sessions across hosts.

### Usage
```
pt agent sessions                          # List recent sessions
pt agent sessions --format json            # JSON output
pt agent sessions --cleanup --older-than 7d # Remove old sessions
pt agent sessions --status <id>            # Single session status
```

### List Output
```json
{
  'sessions': [
    {
      'session_id': 'abc123',
      'host': 'devbox1',
      'state': 'applied',
      'created_at': '2025-01-15T10:00:00Z',
      'candidates': 4,
      'actions_taken': 3
    },
    {
      'session_id': 'def456',
      'host': 'devbox2',
      'state': 'planned',
      'created_at': '2025-01-15T11:00:00Z',
      'candidates': 2,
      'actions_taken': 0
    }
  ]
}
```

### Status Output (Single Session)
```json
{
  'session_id': 'abc123',
  'state': 'interrupted',
  'phase': 'apply',
  'progress': {
    'total_actions': 4,
    'completed_actions': 2,
    'pending_actions': 2
  },
  'resumable': true,
  'resume_command': 'pt agent apply --session abc123 --resume'
}
```

### Session States
- pending: Created but not yet scanned
- scanning: Quick/deep scan in progress
- planned: Plan generated, awaiting action
- applying: Actions being executed
- interrupted: Execution interrupted (resumable)
- applied: Actions completed
- verified: Post-action verification done

### Cleanup
```
pt agent sessions --cleanup --older-than 7d
```
- Removes old sessions while preserving:
  - Outcome rows in Parquet telemetry lake (for prior learning)
  - Append-only retention/audit events (for compliance)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] List sessions with state and metadata
- [ ] Single session status works
- [ ] All session states tracked correctly
- [ ] Resumable sessions identified
- [ ] Cleanup preserves telemetry and audit data
- [ ] JSON and human-readable formats

## Dependencies
- Session model
- Artifact directory management

## Technical Notes
- Session metadata stored in JSON in artifact directory
- State transitions are atomic
- Cleanup uses retention policy from config","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"PinkForge","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T08:54:26.352020165Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-04T03:15:37.299529094Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-6b2","depends_on_id":"process_triage-bwn","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:56Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-6b2","depends_on_id":"process_triage-qje","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:56Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-6b2","depends_on_id":"process_triage-t6lf","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:56Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"process_triage-6jg","title":"Add decision memory tests","description":"## Purpose
Add tests for the decision memory system that learns from user choices.

## Parent Epic
Expanded Test Coverage (process_triage-bgd)

## Key Functions to Test
- `save_decision()`: Persist kill/spare decisions
- `get_past_decision()`: Retrieve previous decision
- `load_decisions_cache()`: Batch load for performance
- `get_cached_decision()`: Fast lookup from cache

## Implementation

### test/test_memory.bats
```bash
#\!/usr/bin/env bats

setup() {
    # Create isolated config directory for each test
    export CONFIG_DIR="${BATS_TEST_TMPDIR}/config"
    export DECISIONS_FILE="${CONFIG_DIR}/decisions.json"
    mkdir -p "$CONFIG_DIR"
    echo '{}' > "$DECISIONS_FILE"
    
    export TEST_MODE=1
    source "${BATS_TEST_DIRNAME}/../pt" 2>/dev/null || true
}

teardown() {
    rm -rf "${BATS_TEST_TMPDIR}/config"
}

#------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# save_decision tests
#------------------------------------------------------------------------------

@test "save_decision: creates valid JSON" {
    skip_if_no_jq
    
    save_decision "kill" "test pattern"
    
    # Should be valid JSON
    jq -e '.' "$DECISIONS_FILE" >/dev/null
}

@test "save_decision: stores kill decision" {
    skip_if_no_jq
    
    save_decision "kill" "bun test"
    
    local stored
    stored=$(jq -r '."bun test"' "$DECISIONS_FILE")
    [[ "$stored" == "kill" ]]
}

@test "save_decision: stores spare decision" {
    skip_if_no_jq
    
    save_decision "spare" "gunicorn"
    
    local stored
    stored=$(jq -r '.gunicorn' "$DECISIONS_FILE")
    [[ "$stored" == "spare" ]]
}

@test "save_decision: overwrites previous decision" {
    skip_if_no_jq
    
    save_decision "kill" "pattern1"
    save_decision "spare" "pattern1"
    
    local stored
    stored=$(jq -r '.pattern1' "$DECISIONS_FILE")
    [[ "$stored" == "spare" ]]
}

@test "save_decision: handles patterns with special characters" {
    skip_if_no_jq
    
    save_decision "kill" "path/to/file --flag=\"value\""
    
    # Should not corrupt JSON
    jq -e '.' "$DECISIONS_FILE" >/dev/null
}

@test "save_decision: handles multiple patterns" {
    skip_if_no_jq
    
    save_decision "kill" "pattern1"
    save_decision "spare" "pattern2"
    save_decision "kill" "pattern3"
    
    local count
    count=$(jq 'length' "$DECISIONS_FILE")
    (( count == 3 ))
}

#------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# get_past_decision tests
#------------------------------------------------------------------------------

@test "get_past_decision: returns stored decision" {
    skip_if_no_jq
    
    echo '{"bun test": "kill"}' > "$DECISIONS_FILE"
    
    local result
    result=$(get_past_decision "bun test")
    [[ "$result" == "kill" ]]
}

@test "get_past_decision: returns unknown for missing pattern" {
    skip_if_no_jq
    
    echo '{}' > "$DECISIONS_FILE"
    
    local result
    result=$(get_past_decision "nonexistent")
    [[ "$result" == "unknown" ]]
}

#------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Cache tests
#------------------------------------------------------------------------------

@test "load_decisions_cache: loads all decisions" {
    skip_if_no_jq
    
    echo '{"p1": "kill", "p2": "spare", "p3": "kill"}' > "$DECISIONS_FILE"
    
    load_decisions_cache
    
    [[ "${DECISION_CACHE[p1]}" == "kill" ]]
    [[ "${DECISION_CACHE[p2]}" == "spare" ]]
    [[ "${DECISION_CACHE[p3]}" == "kill" ]]
}

@test "get_cached_decision: fast lookup" {
    skip_if_no_jq
    
    echo '{"cached_pattern": "spare"}' > "$DECISIONS_FILE"
    load_decisions_cache
    
    local result
    result=$(get_cached_decision "cached_pattern")
    [[ "$result" == "spare" ]]
}

@test "get_cached_decision: returns unknown for missing" {
    skip_if_no_jq
    
    echo '{}' > "$DECISIONS_FILE"
    load_decisions_cache
    
    local result
    result=$(get_cached_decision "missing")
    [[ "$result" == "unknown" ]]
}

#------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Integration tests
#------------------------------------------------------------------------------

@test "decision memory: affects scoring" {
    skip_if_no_jq
    
    # Save a kill decision
    save_decision "kill" "previous pattern"
    
    # Score should be boosted for similar pattern
    # This tests the integration with score_process
}

@test "decision memory: survives script restart" {
    skip_if_no_jq
    
    save_decision "kill" "persistent pattern"
    
    # Clear cache (simulating restart)
    unset DECISION_CACHE
    declare -gA DECISION_CACHE
    DECISION_CACHE_LOADED=false
    
    # Reload and verify
    load_decisions_cache
    [[ "${DECISION_CACHE[persistent pattern]}" == "kill" ]]
}

#------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helper
#------------------------------------------------------------------------------

skip_if_no_jq() {
    if \! command -v jq &>/dev/null; then
        skip "jq not installed"
    fi
}
```

## Success Criteria
- [ ] save_decision creates valid JSON
- [ ] Both kill and spare decisions stored
- [ ] Decisions can be retrieved
- [ ] Cache loading works
- [ ] Fast lookup from cache works
- [ ] Special characters handled safely
- [ ] Unknown patterns return "unknown"

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-6l1","title":"EPIC: Phase 10 - Supervisor Detection","description":"## Overview
Phase 10 implements supervisor detection: identifying processes that are being actively managed by AI agents, IDEs, or automation systems.

## Background
The plan specifies that supervised processes are NEVER auto-killed. Detecting supervision requires examining parent processes, environment variables, IPC channels, and file locks. Known supervisors include Claude, Codex, VS Code, tmux with agent shells, and various CI/CD runners.

## Why It Matters
Killing a supervised process could corrupt an ongoing AI workflow, lose unsaved work, or break a CI pipeline. This is a critical safety mechanism that distinguishes between 'abandoned by user' and 'managed by automation'.

## Phase Scope
1. Supervisor signature database
2. Process ancestry analysis
3. Environment variable inspection
4. IPC and socket connection detection
5. File lock and PID file analysis

## Detection Methods
- **Ancestry**: Parent or ancestor matches supervisor pattern
- **Environment**: Variables like CLAUDE_SESSION_ID, VSCODE_PID, etc.
- **Sockets**: Connected to known supervisor IPC paths
- **Locks**: PID files in known automation directories
- **Timing**: Recent activity correlated with supervisor events

## Known Supervisor Patterns
- AI agents: claude, codex, aider, cursor
- IDEs: code, idea, eclipse, vim (with LSP)
- Terminals: tmux, screen (when hosting agents)
- CI/CD: jenkins, github-runner, gitlab-runner
- Orchestrators: systemd user services, launchd agents

## Output Format
Supervisor detection produces a structured result:
- is_supervised: bool
- supervisor_type: string (agent|ide|ci|orchestrator|unknown)
- supervisor_identity: string (specific supervisor name)
- confidence: float (how certain we are)
- evidence: list of matching signals

## Success Criteria
- All common supervisors detected reliably
- Low false positive rate (non-supervised marked as supervised)
- Fast detection (adds <100ms to scan)
- Extensible pattern database

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-6l1.1","title":"Implement systemd supervisor detection and supervisor-aware actions","description":"## Context
Phase 10 (Supervisor Detection and Supervisor-Aware Actions).

## Problem
Killing a supervised process often causes immediate respawn. The correct action is frequently “stop/restart the unit” rather than killing a PID.

## Scope
- Detect systemd supervision:
  - inspect cgroup paths for systemd slices
  - map PID to unit via `systemctl status <pid>` or cgroup metadata
- Emit supervisor metadata:
  - supervisor_type: systemd
  - unit name
  - stop/restart/reload commands (where safe)
- Integrate with action generation:
  - prefer supervisor action when respawn likely

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Correctly identifies unit for fixture processes (integration tests on systemd-capable env).
- [ ] Generates safe supervisor action commands and includes them in plan output.
- [ ] When a unit is protected/critical, policy blocks supervisor actions by default.

## Test Plan
- Integration tests: mocked `systemctl` output + cgroup fixtures.
- E2E (where possible): run in systemd container/VM and validate mapping.
